source.notes: Kenya uses the same fourth-power law as MoWT/TRL/AASHTO. Standard axle = 80 kN (8,160 kg). The load equivalency factor formula EF = (P/80)^n is universal. Kenya-specific factors derived from RDM 3.3 Section 2.5 using the same methodology as the Uganda MoWT Vol 3 Part I Table 2.3. Axle loads exceeding 13 tonnes (130 kN) require sensitivity analysis per RDM 3.4 Section 2.5.

description: Kenya load equivalency factors for MESA/CESA calculation. Same fourth-power law formula as Uganda but with Kenya-specific terminology (MESA/CESA/DESA/MCESA). Kenya legal single axle limit = 10 tonnes (98 kN).

terminology.MESA: Millions Equivalent Standard Axle Load (= CESA / 1,000,000)
terminology.MCESA: Million Cumulative Equivalent Standard Axles (same as MESA)
terminology.VEF: Vehicle Equivalence Factor (ESA per vehicle passage)
terminology.EF: Equivalency Factor (load equivalency for a single axle)

damage_exponents.description: Recommended damage exponents by pavement type (same as MoWT/TRL approach)

data.description: Load equivalency factors by axle load range for n=3, n=4, and n=4.5. Same formula and values as Uganda MoWT Table 2.3 (both derived from the standard fourth-power law). Presented in both kg and kN ranges.

notes: Kenya uses MESA/CESA terminology throughout -- not ESA/ESAL as used in AASHTO.
The standard axle (80 kN) and fourth-power law are universal across Kenya, Uganda, and TRL methodologies.
Load equivalency factors are derived from the same formula: EF = (P/80)^n. The tabulated values are identical to MoWT Table 2.3 because the formula is the same.
Axle loads exceeding 13 tonnes (130 kN) require sensitivity analysis per RDM 3.4 Section 2.5.
Kenya legal single axle load limit = 10 tonnes (98 kN), same as Uganda. Tandem = 16 tonnes, triaxle = 24 tonnes.
Kenya design traffic classes (TC0.025 to TC150+) are more granular than Uganda MoWT classes (T1-T8).
For traffic > 150 MCESA, performance-based mechanistic design is recommended (RDM 3.4 Section 1.5).
